title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique introduced by Lewis et al. (Facebook AI, 2020) that augments large language models with external knowledge retrieval. Instead of relying solely on parameters, the model retrieves relevant passages from a corpus and conditions its output on them. RAG mitigates hallucinations and stale knowledge in LLMs.

title: BM25 Algorithm
text: BM25 (Best Matching 25) is a probabilistic ranking function used by search engines to estimate the relevance of documents to a given search query. It is the de-facto standard sparse retriever, building on TF-IDF with term frequency saturation and length normalization. BM25 remains a strong baseline often combined with dense embedding retrievers in hybrid search.

title: Dense Retrieval and Embeddings
text: Dense retrieval encodes documents and queries into dense vectors using neural encoders like Sentence-BERT, E5, or BGE. Similarity is computed via cosine or dot product. Unlike BM25 which matches surface terms, dense retrievers capture semantic similarity but can miss exact keyword matches. Hybrid approaches combine both strengths.

title: Reranking with Cross-Encoders
text: Reranking applies a more expensive cross-encoder model to a small candidate set produced by a fast retriever. Cross-encoders jointly process query and document, producing higher relevance accuracy than bi-encoders at the cost of latency. Common rerankers include BGE-reranker, Cohere Rerank, and ColBERT variants.

title: HyDE
text: Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE), proposed by Gao et al. (2022), generates a hypothetical answer with an LLM and uses its embedding for retrieval. The hypothetical answer shares vocabulary distribution with relevant documents, improving semantic matching when query-document style differs.

title: Self-RAG
text: Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2023) trains an LLM to emit reflection tokens that decide when to retrieve and assess the relevance and support of retrieved passages. This adaptive behavior reduces unnecessary retrievals and filters out low-quality passages, improving factuality and efficiency.

title: CRAG (Corrective RAG)
text: Corrective RAG (Yan et al., 2024) evaluates retrieved documents with a lightweight retrieval evaluator and triggers corrective actions: confident, ambiguous, or incorrect. When confidence is low, it falls back to web search; when ambiguous, it knowledge-refines via decomposition and recomposition. CRAG improves robustness to imperfect retrieval.

title: GraphRAG
text: GraphRAG, released by Microsoft in 2024, builds a knowledge graph from documents using LLMs to extract entities and relationships. Community detection clusters the graph into themes, each summarized for retrieval. GraphRAG excels at holistic, summary-style questions but has high indexing cost compared to chunk-based RAG.

title: RAPTOR
text: RAPTOR (Sarthi et al., 2024) builds a recursive abstractive tree over document chunks by repeated clustering and LLM-based summarization. At query time, retrieval traverses multiple tree depths, mixing fine-grained details with abstract summaries. RAPTOR shines on long, multi-document QA.

title: Contextual Retrieval
text: Contextual Retrieval, introduced by Anthropic in September 2024, prepends a short LLM-generated context blurb to each chunk before embedding. This helps disambiguate chunks that lose meaning when isolated from their parent document. Anthropic reports a 49% average reduction in retrieval failure rate, with prompt caching keeping preprocessing cost low.

title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion
text: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines rankings from multiple retrievers by summing 1/(k+rank) scores. Because it uses ranks rather than raw scores, RRF safely fuses heterogeneous systems like BM25 and dense vectors. It is the standard hybrid-search fusion algorithm.

title: RAGAS Evaluation
text: RAGAS is an open-source Python framework for evaluating RAG pipelines using LLMs as judges. Core metrics include faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall. RAGAS integrates with LangChain and LlamaIndex; evaluation itself consumes LLM tokens, so cost monitoring is essential.

title: Qdrant
text: Qdrant is an open-source vector search engine written in Rust. It supports payload filtering, hybrid dense-sparse search, and runs easily via Docker. Qdrant strikes a balance between Chroma's simplicity and Elasticsearch's feature depth, making it a popular default for production RAG.

title: Prompt Caching
text: Prompt caching reuses processed prefixes (system prompts, long contexts) across requests to reduce token cost and latency. Anthropic introduced explicit cache_control markers in August 2024; OpenAI offers automatic prefix caching. In RAG, caching system prompts and frequently used documents can cut cost by 50-90%.

title: Multi-Query Retrieval
text: Multi-query retrieval generates several reformulations of a user query with an LLM and runs retrieval for each. Results are merged via RRF or score aggregation. The technique boosts recall when user queries are short or ambiguous. LangChain's MultiQueryRetriever is a canonical implementation.
